questions.specification: A team of designers is tasked with reimagining a public park information kiosk that will serve tourists, local residents, elderly users, and people with various disabilities. The park authority wants the kiosk to support a high volume of users from diverse backgrounds and abilities, while maintaining simplicity and elegance in the design. They are analysing the following features.

parts.content:
The screen features bright, clearly labelled icons with a black background.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2c2Ur6j-KuE1yZEnnRQgQ) (Fig 1)

Outline two reasons why **Figure 1**’s design improves accessibility for elderly users.
parts.markscheme:
- Large icons are easier for users with impaired vision to recognize. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High-contrast colours reduce eye strain and enhance readability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award <marks>1 mark</marks> per appropriate reason with a clear link to accessibility, max <marks>2 marks</marks>.*

parts.content:
Physical control panel with a rotary dial, Braille labels, and audio jack for headphones



![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/4f0c5d33-bb0d-4218-93c1-8117f4a5ea7f)

Identify one feature in **Figure 2** that supports users with visual impairments.
parts.markscheme:
- The inclusion of Braille labels allows users with limited vision to identify controls. 

*Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for any single correct identification of a visual accessibility feature.*

parts.content: Compare the strategies used in **Figure 1** and **Figure 2** to support users with different impairments.
parts.markscheme:
- Figure 1 focuses on visual accessibility through interface design. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Figure 2 addresses both tactile and auditory accessibility. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Figure 1 benefits a broad range of users, while Figure 2 targets more specific needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both figures apply inclusive design but through different sensory approaches. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award <marks>1 mark</marks> per clear comparative point, up to <marks>4 marks</marks>.*

parts.content:
Users can press a button to lower and tilt the screen to their preferred height.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b0e62be9-3703-4022-9342-d5d07067d6e8)

Explain how **Figure 3** demonstrates inclusive design principles.
parts.markscheme:
- The adjustable height makes the kiosk usable by both seated and standing users. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows customization based on user preference and physical needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensures equal access regardless of a user’s mobility status. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award <marks>1 mark</marks> per well-explained link to inclusivity, max <marks>3 marks</marks>*

parts.content: Discuss how designing for extremes has been applied across all three figures in this kiosk system.
parts.markscheme:
- Figure 3 accommodates wheelchair users, an extreme case of mobility. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Figure 2 includes Braille and audio features for users with full visual loss. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These extremes still provide benefits for many users (e.g. those pushing strollers or with partial sight). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Designers avoid relying on the 50th percentile “average” user profile. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Strategies enhance universal usability while supporting specific groups. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The design anticipates user diversity across age, ability, and language. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award <marks>1 mark</marks> per thoughtful application of the “design for extremes” strategy, max <marks>6 marks</marks>*

questions.specification: A smartphone that includes voice control benefits which of the following groups?

options.content: All users, including those with impairments

parts.content: Explain why inclusive design is important in public infrastructure.
parts.markscheme:
- Ensures that everyone, including wheelchair users or elderly individuals, can access services <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces social exclusion by removing barriers in everyday environments <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Meets legal obligations under accessibility laws <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Promotes dignity and independence for all users <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each well-explained reason up to [4 max].</note>

questions.specification: An electric toothbrush with larger grips and single-button use reflects:

Which element of this museum exhibit reflects inclusive design principles?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfqm1BlceqzJtlw5FXNRBPRClImtyUP0w5ODagqXBFHyyw1H_o8Nv2BuiV4oXE2yOnUAXfhSSY5_mGlGvDioEyFliRrvzz1kUcqUY8zDh-WV4ydgcc0Pj1ZKpKsrOHCxpL2AxO8?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXevW1_GoRKj08WGl1tRfS-vXg3TB2lewvA8-foiJYW6NkZNCdBepXThqUSeitEH8Ymh3gWTb_LRFyDQaB3wPzKZEmXVVBjvnH-VgTYfecdw9taogMYSA0lI4zzmlqaW1-0sqkBP?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Interactive digital screens with customizable brightness
Text-heavy visual panels in multiple fonts
Tactile surfaces and audio descriptions for the blind

questions.specification:
Why might designing to the 50th percentile be inappropriate for this product?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXe7mKGh1G_TqxXkwQDY7AfAqj4BsrnV73J41BnLVI_IAhTF6MWeb0gBXo2gwVuc-9qIEcwK6otvuJBPAzNjSgpjjojI9X4KpX3Dx8RMxD-ZmBktgZVHLy8gKaUcbMMPCRt5rba4PA?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: It excludes both smaller and taller users
options.markscheme: Designing for the 50th percentile (the average) fails to accommodate users at the extremes of the size range. In ergonomics, products like office chairs must usually accommodate the 5th to 95th percentiles to ensure a proper fit for both smaller/shorter and larger/taller individuals.
Perceived luxury is typically determined by aesthetics and material quality rather than percentile design.
The use of anthropometric data does not restrict the variety of materials that can be used in a product.
Percentile design is focused on physical fit and comfort, not the visibility of the product during use.

questions.specification: Why might inclusive design be avoided in competitive product markets?

options.content: It can increase development time and cost
It may require redesigning established products and processes

questions.specification: Which of the following is a key challenge in applying inclusive design principles?

options.content: Meeting all user needs can be impractical
Most users prefer exclusive, niche products

questions.specification:
What inclusive feature is evident in this bus design?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXeA3-nBE_X04HiLmRrh-nP_86BqPAKcVsKlPGocsX8ANsTBmmM74CS70OkG_ncaR0JIrkj3CaGig8bPiX9_fihpfVeiRgS-3biKfIp7zpEWQ1t4B4PbPS3z4KdI660JkWmYoL7X?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

A new city tram system is being developed with the goal of making public transport more inclusive. The design team is focused on ensuring that users with a wide range of physical, sensory, and cognitive needs can board and navigate the tram comfortably, while also addressing concerns from stakeholders about cost, usability, and performance for the average commuter.

parts.content:
A modern low-floor tram entrance allows for level boarding from the platform.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/wByWMYi1eekkwT1F_wtbE) (Fig 1)

Identify one design feature in **Figure 1** that supports users with limited mobility.
parts.markscheme:
- The retractable ramp allows wheelchair users to board independently. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for any feature clearly linked to mobility accessibility.*

parts.content:
Panels are duplicated at different heights to serve passengers with limited mobility.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/OnfXykDPuJyUA3XxXvXTz) (Fig 2)

Explain how the design in **Figure 2** reflects the principle of universal usability.
parts.markscheme:
- Buttons placed at multiple heights accommodate both standing and seated users. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The same interaction method works for all passengers. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- No user is excluded due to their physical height or mobility level. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid explanation related to inclusivity, max [3].*

parts.content:
Each stop is clearly marked on a scrolling LED bar and announced aloud in more than one language.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/R3MP5b9P5942r5gca5a3e) (Fig 3)

Outline two ways **Figure 3** supports people with sensory impairments.
parts.markscheme:
- The audio announcements assist those with visual impairments. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The LED route display assists passengers who are hard of hearing. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid accessibility support, max [2].*

parts.content: Compare the approaches in **Figure 1** and **Figure 3** in terms of inclusive design strategy.
parts.markscheme:
- Figure 1 demonstrates physical accessibility through ramps and handrails. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Figure 3 uses sensory features like sound and visual cues to broaden access. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both support different user needs but target the widest audience possible. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Figure 1 primarily aids physical limitations, while Figure 3 addresses cognitive/sensory needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid comparative point between physical and sensory accessibility, max [4].*

parts.content: Discuss the benefits and trade-offs of using a “design for extremes” strategy in this tram system.
parts.markscheme:
- Ensures access for individuals with severe mobility, vision, or cognitive challenges. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May lead to features that improve usability for all (e.g. easier-to-read signage). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May increase cost or complexity due to additional systems (e.g. multilingual audio technology). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Could reduce efficiency for average users if design sacrifices speed or space. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Designers must balance inclusivity with practicality and operational throughput. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps future-proof systems as populations age and mobility needs shift. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per relevant benefit or trade-off with reasoning, max [6].*

questions.specification: Why might inclusive design **NOT** always be implemented in products?

Why is this public bench an example of inclusive design?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/8c6b0eb5-d02c-4288-aca2-322a4f5fad21.jpeg)

options.content: It prevents vandalism through fixed anchoring
options.markscheme: Vandalism prevention relates to durability and maintenance, which are aspects of physical resilience, but not specifically 'inclusive design' which focuses on user accessibility.

options.content: It includes aesthetic carvings reflecting cultural heritage
options.markscheme: While cultural reflection can be a part of emotional design, inclusive design primarily focuses on functional accessibility for a wide range of users, particularly those with physical limitations.

options.content: It is made from recycled composite materials
options.markscheme: Using recycled materials is a principle of sustainable or green design, not inclusive design.

options.content: It accommodates users of varying mobility with supportive features
options.markscheme: Inclusive design aims to make environments and products accessible to the widest possible range of people. Features like armrests and backrests provide essential physical support for users with limited mobility or strength, such as the elderly, when sitting or standing up.

parts.content: Outline limitations of designing for the "average" user.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that the 50th percentile may not represent the needs of the majority of people. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- State that this approach can exclude individuals at the extremes of height, mobility, or cognitive ability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explain that it leads to poor usability for those outside the narrow target range. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Identify that it may create legal and ethical concerns regarding accessibility and inclusivity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
What limitation of inclusive design is demonstrated in this playground?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXcRJUGOSxen9iQzbM_1FvPUQrwbqkXIDioTioBBObESCz0fhnY4gNCKQussaWOeej9DUsEY0azHgn2oWHQpJZbCyI3H_Grcd8Ff19eH-VzCmvz1G5ggIUSG8FFnU4YtQiSzovCnJQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: High cost and spatial demand for accessible equipment

questions.specification: Designing for extremes often leads to:

options.content: Products that serve only niche customers
Unusable interfaces for the general public
Solutions that are impractical for most users

parts.content: Describe the "design for extremes" strategy.
parts.markscheme:
- Focuses on designing for users at either end of the user spectrum (e.g. very tall, blind, young children) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensures usability for those with physical, sensory, or cognitive impairments <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Often results in products that benefit the wider population as well <marks>1 mark</marks>
- For example, kerb cuts help both wheelchair users and people with strollers or luggage <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Design for extremes is especially useful when:

options.content: Tailoring niche products for luxury brands
Creating inclusive solutions through edge-case needs
Reducing the lifespan of a product

questions.specification: A national train operator is upgrading its stations to meet modern standards of accessibility. Their goal is to ensure that passengers with visual, auditory, mobility, and cognitive impairments can use the facilities independently and safely.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: Tactile surfaces with raised dots or bars are installed to warn visually impaired users of the platform edge.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/aZCs4sBOO0lVcqkXXzE1U)

Identify one inclusive design feature in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
● The tactile paving alerts visually impaired users of a platform drop. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for any clearly identified inclusive feature of the paving.*

parts.content:
Figure 2: Large screens display real-time train times while automated announcements repeat schedule changes.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/11ddc1df-266a-4f92-8fda-10e6f8d8613d)


Outline two benefits of combining audio and visual systems as seen in **Figure 2**.
parts.markscheme:
Provides access to information for both hearing- and visually-impaired users. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Reduces reliance on a single sensory channel, improving reliability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clearly outlined advantage of multi-sensory design, max [2].*

parts.content:
Figure 3: The station includes gently sloped ramps and wider gates suitable for wheelchair users, luggage, and prams.



![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/cd0d9dc3-bd92-4ce9-815b-43c75a372a9b)

Explain how the wide ticket gates in **Figure 3** support inclusive design.
parts.markscheme:
● Accommodate users in wheelchairs or with guide dogs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Provide space for travellers with prams or large luggage. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Reduce stress for users who may need more time to pass through. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clearly explained benefit related to accessibility, max [3].*

parts.content: Compare the inclusive design approaches in **Figure 1** and **Figure 3**.
parts.markscheme:
● Figure 1 supports users with visual impairments through tactile cues. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Figure 3 focuses on physical mobility, enabling independent movement. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Both features improve safety and independence in high-traffic areas. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Tactile paving is passive, while gate design requires spatial and mechanical planning. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid comparison, max [4].*

parts.content: Suggest one inclusive design improvement the station could make for users with autism.
parts.markscheme:
● Introduce quiet waiting zones with reduced noise and lighting. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Use colour-coded signage to simplify navigation and reduce anxiety. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per relevant suggestion that supports neurodiverse users, max [2].*

parts.content: Evaluate the long-term benefits of inclusive transport infrastructure for society.
parts.markscheme:
● Encourages independence for elderly and disabled individuals. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Increases transport accessibility for families and tourists. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Improves station flow by reducing user confusion or delays. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Promotes equal opportunity and social participation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Reduces need for staff assistance, lowering operational strain. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Enhances public perception of fairness and innovation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per well-developed point demonstrating long-term inclusive benefits, max [6].*

questions.specification: Which of the following is **NOT** typically a feature of inclusive design?

options.content: Ease of use for diverse populations

questions.specification: Which principle is most closely associated with inclusive design?

parts.content: Evaluate the impact of inclusive design on commercial product success.
parts.markscheme:
- **Expands target market**: Catering to a wider range of users, such as the elderly or individuals with disabilities, increases the potential customer base and sales volume. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Builds customer loyalty**: Providing accessible and user-friendly designs satisfies diverse needs, leading to repeat purchases and strong brand advocacy. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Enhances brand image**: Positioning the brand as socially responsible and inclusive attracts ethical consumers and improves overall public perception. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Improves overall usability**: Solutions designed for extreme users often enhance the user experience for the mainstream market, leading to higher product satisfaction scores. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Drives innovation**: The constraints of inclusive design often lead to innovative uses of materials or interfaces that provide a competitive advantage. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Long-term profitability**: Although initial research and development costs may be higher, the expanded market share leads to greater long-term financial gains. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Regulatory compliance**: Meeting international accessibility standards reduces the risk of legal challenges and allows access to restricted markets, such as government contracts. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Word-of-mouth marketing**: Satisfied underserved groups often have strong community networks, leading to effective organic recommendations. <marks>A1</marks>

- **Reduces product returns**: Intuitive and accessible designs reduce consumer frustration and errors, significantly lowering the rate of returns and associated costs. <marks>A1</marks>

9 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each commercial impact clearly linked to inclusive design up to [9 max].</note>

questions.specification:
What design principle is used to make this kitchen tool accessible to users with arthritis?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXehyPtAUC7SEbRbt2mwCHkjZQPrJxGdoMK81yoeLP8ntSQbjxFFBEQ04eUXO53fp87GJ_7D0HdasgLsPCBSdMCQ0DI4e-KYhwyVRTj2euCBLE-vPb9Gb-vkK79amHiqb42iC7tAtQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C. Design in context > C1.2 Inclusive design > C1.2.1 Inclusive design
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C1.2.1 Inclusive design
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks about making a product accessible to a user group with specific physical needs (arthritis), which is the core objective of Inclusive Design.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A. Design in theory > A1.1 Ergonomics > A1.1.1 Ergonomics
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A1.1.1 Ergonomics
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The correct response (large soft-grip handles) is a direct application of ergonomics, focusing on the physical interaction between the tool and the user's hand.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C. Design in context > C1.2 Inclusive design > C1.2.3 Design for extremes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C1.2.3 Design for extremes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Arthritis users represent an 'extreme' in terms of grip strength and dexterity; designing for this group ensures the product is usable for a wider range of people.

options.markscheme: Large handles require less grip strength and 'soft-grip' materials increase friction, reducing the pressure needed from the user's joints. This is a core application of ergonomic and inclusive design for users with limited dexterity or joint pain.
Heat resistance is a safety feature for cooking tools but does not specifically address the mechanical accessibility challenges faced by users with arthritis.
Standard plastic molds are used to reduce manufacturing costs through economies of scale, but they do not contribute to the ergonomic accessibility of the product.
While a dual-function mechanism adds versatility, it may actually increase complexity and difficulty for an arthritic user if it requires more complex manipulation.

questions.specification: Which best describes the philosophy of universal design?

options.content: Designing products accessible to all, by default
Tailoring products to a specific demographic

parts.content: Identify two impairments that inclusive design specifically seeks to accommodate.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify a physical impairment, such as limited mobility <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify a sensory impairment, such as hearing or vision loss <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] mark for each distinct impairment category identified, up to a maximum of [2]. Other valid categories include cognitive or intellectual impairments.</note>

questions.specification: An airport in a major city is undergoing renovations to improve accessibility across all terminals. The design team is focused on making the airport more welcoming and easier to navigate for people of all ages and abilities. They are also discussing when inclusive design may not be realistic and whether a “design for extremes” approach might be more effective in certain areas.

parts.content:
A raised tactile guide with braille labels allows visually impaired users to understand the layout.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8SN83S4xbLXvL46Ku9CSr) (Fig 1)

Identify one inclusive design feature shown in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
● The raised tactile floor map with braille supports users with visual impairments. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for a valid feature that reflects inclusive design.*

parts.content: Explain how **Figure 1** supports universally accessible design.
parts.markscheme:
● Users with limited or no vision can still understand spatial layout. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Braille and tactile elements don’t interfere with use by the general population. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● The map ensures independence in navigation for all users. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per explanation showing how the design suits a wide range of users, up to [3 max].*

parts.content:
These self-service stations are designed with the average adult in mind.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ae54f0a9-c415-4d4d-8d81-b45dec0a8f0b)

Discuss why it may not always be possible to apply inclusive design, as shown in **Figure 2**.
parts.markscheme:
● Space and budget constraints may limit adjustability in kiosk height. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Designing for everyone can lead to compromises that serve no one well. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● The 50th percentile adult may be used to optimise speed for most users. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Inclusive redesigns could reduce throughput in high-traffic areas. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Retrofits may not fully accommodate all impairments. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Technology limitations can also constrain inclusive features. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per well-reasoned point about practical design limits, max [6].*

parts.content:
A redesigned restroom space that accommodates people with mobility aids and carers.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d508d07f-5440-4f60-9874-01eaa299c9f3)

Outline two physical accessibility improvements shown in **Figure 3**.
parts.markscheme:
● The wide automatic doorway accommodates wheelchair users. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● The adult-sized changing table supports people with complex care needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid improvement based on the figure, up to [2 max].*

parts.content: Compare how inclusive design is approached in **Figure 2** versus **Figure 3**.
parts.markscheme:
● Figure 2 targets the average user by using standard height kiosks. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Figure 3 accommodates extremes by supporting individuals with mobility impairments. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Figure 2 may exclude users in wheelchairs or children. <marks>1 mark</marks>

● Figure 3 shows a broader application of inclusive design principles. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid comparison based on inclusive principles, max [4].*

questions.specification: What is a key benefit of designing for extremes?
Which of the following is NOT a common barrier to inclusive design?
Inclusive design is generally most effective when:

options.content: Focused solely on the physically impaired
Used to replace existing accessible systems
Integrated early in the design process

questions.specification: A public transport ticketing machine with tactile buttons and voice instructions demonstrates:
A designer prioritizing inclusive design would most likely **AVOID**:
Inclusive design aims to benefit:

options.content: A broad user base, regardless of ability

questions.specification: Which strategy is often used when inclusive design cannot meet all needs?
What is the primary goal of inclusive design?

options.content: Focus exclusively on the needs of disabled users
Reduce costs through minimal design features
Cater to the widest possible range of users
Maximize aesthetic appeal for specific users

questions.specification: The use of adjustable-height desks in schools demonstrates:
A product designed for people with visual impairments but usable by all is an example of:

options.markscheme: Inclusive design refers to the design of mainstream products and/or services that are accessible to, and usable by, as many people as reasonably possible, including those with disabilities, without the need for specialized adaptation.
Exclusive design focuses on a specific segment of users and may deliberately exclude others who do not meet certain criteria or abilities.
Aesthetic-led design prioritizes the visual appeal and form of a product over its usability or accessibility.
Ergonomic limitation refers to constraints in design based on the physical or cognitive capabilities of users, but does not define the inclusive intent of a product.

questions.specification:
What strategy is reflected in the creation of this universal remote control?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXeDy2qUQGv0IFgSRy7udt6rDi4RbY3JxfXFWL9ZEX-C6cTtgQ8tHn0uDqAIpZJVP0gJVDDFwSjnLBYhDX5ZvTF_eYbRoFQep5AZtXQiTfulCQR2ns68dGirsbb_1ppzhDuGrXGU?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.markscheme: The remote control shown is a simplified version of a standard remote, featuring large buttons and a limited interface. This design strategy prioritizes ease of use and comprehension, particularly for elderly users or those with cognitive or physical impairments (Inclusive Design).

questions.specification:
Why is the design of this door handle effective for inclusive access?

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/83fc1afb-21b8-41c5-b163-3c3262fafb9f)

options.markscheme: The material finish relates to aesthetics or durability rather than specific accessibility for inclusive design.
While hygienic, preventing dirt accumulation is not the primary reason this design is effective for inclusive access.

options.content: Requires less wrist and grip strength
options.markscheme: Lever-style handles are an inclusive design feature because they can be operated with a simple downward motion, requiring significantly less grip and wrist rotation than a traditional doorknob. This makes them accessible to users with limited dexterity or strength, such as those with arthritis.
Architectural matching is an aesthetic concern and does not contribute to the inclusivity or accessibility of the handle.

questions.specification:
Why might this ATM's layout disadvantage some users?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/0519e85c-dec4-425b-8ca0-2a27badebbc1.jpeg)

options.content: The cash slot is centrally positioned
options.markscheme: A centrally positioned cash slot is typically an ergonomic design choice and does not disadvantage specific user groups.

options.content: The numeric keypad is in Braille
options.markscheme: Braille characters on a keypad are an accessibility feature that assists visually impaired users, providing an advantage rather than a disadvantage.
Auto-generating a receipt is a procedural feature that does not relate to physical layout disadvantages for specific user groups.

options.content: The touchscreen height excludes seated users
options.markscheme: If a touchscreen is positioned too high, it creates a physical barrier for users in wheelchairs (seated users) or individuals of shorter stature, preventing them from interacting with the system.

parts.content: Define the term "inclusive design."
parts.markscheme:
- Inclusive design is a design philosophy that aims to create products and environments usable by as many people as possible <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It considers users of all ages and abilities, including those with impairments <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>
Award [1] for accurate definition and [1] for relevance to diverse user needs.
</note>

questions.specification:
Which feature of this ticket machine promotes inclusive design?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfSu17zP4XI2rmDSpmJIvt-gPje-W4u-_HfXQiqCjZrGya9ZRFKSSARrrAzbjfmHjINmBhMH5OmI77aQvbyQ56bK6codIPHGJMqd6kZHRjVK1Cyt7oC9137BBRaH3ezpwjtKx6iaQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.markscheme: A backlit slot improves visibility for all users but does not specifically address the needs of users with sensory or cognitive impairments as effectively as tactile or audio systems.

options.content: Raised tactile buttons and audio guidance
options.markscheme: Inclusive design aims to make products accessible to the widest possible range of people. Raised tactile buttons allow users with visual impairments to navigate the interface by touch, while audio guidance provides spoken instructions, assisting those who cannot see the screen.
Restricting transactions to contactless methods can exclude users who do not have access to such technology, which is a form of exclusive rather than inclusive design.
The physical shape of the machine's housing is a general aesthetic and structural feature and does not specifically promote inclusive design.
